text.title: Specification Template: Rules and Fill Order
text.summary: Rules for correctly filling the spec.md template: fill order, required sections, what belongs in spec vs design, MoSCoW on FRs and NFRs, status gate, and anti-patterns.

rule.statement: Every spec.md must contain all required sections: Reference Documents, Overview, Problem Statement, User Personas (minimum 2), User Stories, Functional Requirements table (FR-X), Non-Functional Requirements table (NFR-X), Acceptance Criteria, Out of Scope, Assumptions and Constraints, and Dependencies.
rule.rationale: Missing sections cause design agents to make ungrounded decisions. Out of Scope is the most commonly omitted section and the most important for preventing scope creep.

list.item: Reference Documents — links to constitution.md and related done/ specs
Overview — 2-3 sentences describing the feature and its purpose
Problem Statement — Who, Problem, Impact, Solution (high-level only), Success Metric
User Personas — minimum 2, each with Role, Goal, Pain Points, Context
User Stories — "As a / I want / So that" with US-X IDs and MoSCoW priority
Functional Requirements — FR-X table with Requirement, Traces To (US-X), Priority (MoSCoW)
Non-Functional Requirements — NFR-X table with Category, Requirement, Target (numeric), Priority
Acceptance Criteria — AC-X in Given-When-Then format, linked to FR-X
Out of Scope — explicit table of named excluded features with reason and timeline
Assumptions and Constraints — technical and business constraints
Dependencies — internal (other features) and external (APIs, services)

rule.statement: Spec contains WHAT and WHY only. Any sentence describing HOW something works belongs in design.md, not spec.md.
rule.rationale: Implementation details in spec.md become stale when design decisions change, causing version drift between spec and design.

example.code: FR-003: System SHALL use bcrypt with cost factor 12 for password hashing.

example.note: Correct spec requirement — what, not how
example.code: FR-003: System SHALL store passwords using a cryptographically secure one-way hash that is resistant to brute-force attacks. [Must]

rule.statement: Assign MoSCoW priority (Must/Should/Could/Won't) to every FR and NFR. Unclassified requirements default to "Must" during implementation, causing scope creep.
rule.rationale: Without explicit priority, executor agents treat all requirements as equally urgent and implement Could requirements before Should requirements.

rule.statement: Every non-functional requirement must include a specific numeric target and measurement unit. "Fast" and "responsive" are not valid NFR values.
rule.rationale: NFRs without numbers are unenforceable in testing and cannot be validated by automated checks.

example.code: NFR-001: System should have fast response times.
NFR-001: API response time SHALL be < 500ms at p95 under 100 concurrent users. [Must]

rule.statement: Spec must reach Approved status before design begins. Never hand a Draft or Review spec to an executor agent.
rule.rationale: Design decisions made against unapproved requirements cause rework when requirements change during the review cycle.

antipattern.description: Out of Scope section lists only generic exclusions ("mobile app", "internationalization").
antipattern.reason: Out of Scope must name specific features that were discussed in requirements gathering and explicitly rejected with reasons. Generic exclusions do not prevent scope creep.

antipattern.description: User stories without "so that" benefit clause.
antipattern.reason: The benefit clause is where business value lives. Stories without it cannot be prioritized against each other.

antipattern.description: Single persona for a multi-user feature.
antipattern.reason: Different personas have different goals and pain points. A single persona produces requirements that serve one audience and silently exclude others.

text.reference: Complete spec.md template with all required sections
Bash script to verify every FR-X references a US and has an AC
